Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd (in compulsory liquidation)

Facts.Text: Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd was incorporated on 2011-04-08.
From incorporation, Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd was in the business of chartering ships, tankers and vessels, and their management and operations.
From 2011-04-08, Wee See Boon was the local resident director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
From 2011-04-08, Peter Christian Harren was a director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
From 2011-04-08, Martin Harren was a director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
Peter Christian Harren and Martin Harren are German nationals residing in Germany.
All three directors, Wee See Boon, Peter Christian Harren and Martin Harren, were signatories of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's DBS bank accounts.
Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's former immediate holding company was Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.
Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's former ultimate holding company was HPS International Holding GmbH.
Wee See Boon was a director of Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.
Wee See Boon was a signatory of Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd's bank account.
By the end of 2017, Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd had stopped generating revenue.

Between 2018-12-12 and 2018-12-27, Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd made six payments totaling US$1,461,000 to Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd as partial repayment of a loan from Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd to Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.

Between 2018-12-12 and 2018-12-27, Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd made payments totaling US$1,472,500 to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG, comprising US$790,500 to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and US$682,000 to HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG.

Service agreements between Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd and HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and between Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG were signed by Wee See Boon and dated 2018-10-01.
At the time of the service agreements dated 2018-10-01, Martin Harren was a director of HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG.

On 2020-09-11, Luther LLP sent a letter on behalf of the three former directors of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd stating that the service providers reserved the right to settle group-wide costs at a later stage and that the costs of services were settled in 2018, describing this as a usual setup for companies in the business.

On 2021-06-15, I.N.C Law LLC sent a letter on behalf of Wee See Boon stating in response to question 19 that it is not uncommon to distribute project-related costs among project sponsors at a later point in time.
As of 2018-12-31, Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd had net liabilities of US$1,132,751.
As of 2018-12-31, Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd owed OIG Giant 1 Pte Ltd S$1,805,568.10.

According to Wee See Boon, the payments to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG related to services furnished to Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd from around 2014-03-01 to around 2016-09-01 concerning the vessel Blue Giant in the Gulf of Mexico.
On 2020-04-19, OIG Giant 1 Pte Ltd filed a winding up application against Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.

On 2021-12-01, Luther LLP wrote that HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG considered their claims against Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd to be well founded but were prepared to repay sums if the payments were seen as preferential treatment over other creditors under Singapore law and to justify their claims in the same way as other creditors.
On 2021-12-23, HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG refunded US$1,472,500 to Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.

Wee See Boon described his primary responsibilities as local resident director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd as handling paperwork with auditors, agencies and regulatory authorities in Singapore.

Wee See Boon stated that the German directors, Peter Christian Harren and/or Martin Harren, had full management and conduct of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's business operations and financial matters.

On 2022-03-01, the defendant's legal representatives stated that for any restitution of interest, the applicable rate should be the then-prevailing fixed deposit interest rate of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's bank of 0.8% per annum.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether payments totaling US$1,472,500 made by Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd in December 2018 to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG, and the associated claimed additional damages for interest and investigation-related costs, should result in personal monetary responsibility of former director Wee See Boon.
Party_Details.Rule: Company directors are comply with statutory and general law standards governing directors' conduct, including acting in the interests of the company and exercising reasonable care, skill and diligence, and transactions made by a company before winding up may be avoidance rules concerning preferential payments to certain creditors.
Party_Details.Application: Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd made substantial payments in December 2018 to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG after receiving loan repayments from its holding company, at a time when it had ceased generating revenue and had significant liabilities including a large debt to OIG Giant 1 Pte Ltd. The payments were connected to service agreements signed in October 2018 and to earlier services relating to the vessel Blue Giant, and the payee entities shared a common director with the plaintiff's group. These events occurred before the winding up application filed by OIG Giant 1 Pte Ltd and before the later refund of the US$1,472,500 by HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court characterised the December 2018 payments totaling US$1,472,500 to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG as unfair preference payments under the applicable insolvency provisions, noting that the recipients were creditors under the service agreements, that the company was insolvent or rendered insolvent by the payments, that the payments placed those creditors in a better position than in a winding up, that the payments occurred within the relevant two-year period for associates, and that a statutory presumption of desire to prefer applied and was not rebutted. However, the court focused on whether the defendant director, Wee See Boon, breached fiduciary or statutory duties in relation to these payments and the claimed additional damages. The judge accepted that Wee See Boon, as a director, owed core fiduciary duties and a duty of care, skill and diligence, but distinguished between duties and remedies. The plaintiff's case was premised on alleged breaches of core fiduciary duties and sought equitable compensation for additional damages comprising interest on the principal sum and investigation and winding up costs. The court examined the plaintiff's allegations that the defendant failed to determine whether the payments were permissible and failed to ascertain whether the other directors complied with their duties and the company's constitution when facilitating the payments. The defendant's position was that he was a local resident director with limited administrative responsibilities, that the German directors managed operations and finances, and that he was unaware of the payments at the material time, learning of them only later from the finance manager, who informed him that the payments were approved by the German finance department via an authorised token. The court accepted that the defendant did not in fact check the permissibility of the payments or the conduct of the other directors, but held that the key question was whether he knew or ought to have known of the payments. The plaintiff relied on audited financial statements, the service agreements, and the 11 September 2020 letter to argue that the defendant could not claim ignorance. The judge found that these materials showed awareness of other creditors and group arrangements but did not establish that the defendant knew or should have known that the specific payments were made. The court noted that the audited financial statements indicated the debt to OIG Giant 1 Pte Ltd but did not record debts to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG, and accepted the explanation in the 11 September 2020 letter that group-wide costs were settled later, rejecting the suggestion that debts were deliberately omitted. The judge held that even if the debts should have been recorded earlier, the plaintiff had not shown a causal link between any defect in financial statements and the payments or the claimed additional damages. The court distinguished Living the Link Pte Ltd (in creditors' voluntary liquidation) v Tan Lay Tin Tina, where the director had procured the undue preferences, and held that the inference of breach of fiduciary duty from an undue preference was not automatic in a case where the director's alleged wrongdoing was a failure to prevent payments he did not know about. The court also considered Ho Pak Kim Realty Co Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Ho Soo Fong, where a director was found liable despite claiming to be a sleeping director, but found that case inapplicable because there the director was found to be aware of the relevant transactions and related-party debts, whereas here the evidence did not show that Wee See Boon knew or should have known of the payments. On the duty of care, skill and diligence, the court noted the absence of specific pleading or evidence as to what steps were reasonably expected of the defendant in his role, given that he was the sole local director with limited administrative responsibilities and that the German directors controlled operations and finances. The judge referred to Prima Bulkship Pte Ltd v Lim Say Wan, recognising that the nature of a local resident director's appointment affects the extent of expected involvement. The court concluded that the plaintiff had not shown that the defendant's lack of knowledge of the payments was unreasonable or that he failed to meet the objective standard of care in the circumstances. Because no breach of fiduciary or statutory duty was established, the foundation for the plaintiff's claim for additional damages, including interest and investigation and petition costs, failed. The court therefore dismissed the application and the plaintiff's prayers for damages against the defendant, noting that the defendant's earlier application to convert the originating summons to a writ for a full trial with oral evidence had been opposed by the plaintiff, and that the court was constrained to decide on affidavits and documents without cross-examination or evidence from the German directors.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wee See Boon

Facts.Text: From 2011-04-08 to 2020-03-26, Wee See Boon was the local resident director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
From 2011-04-08, Wee See Boon was a director of Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.
From 2011-04-08, Wee See Boon was a signatory of the bank account of Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.
From 2011-04-08, Wee See Boon was a signatory of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's DBS bank accounts.

Wee See Boon described his primary responsibility as local resident director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd as ensuring necessary paperwork with auditors, agencies and regulatory authorities in Singapore.

Wee See Boon stated that the German directors, Peter Christian Harren and/or Martin Harren, had full management and conduct of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's business operations and financial matters.
On 2018-10-01, Wee See Boon signed a service agreement between Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd and HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co.
On 2018-10-01, Wee See Boon signed a service agreement between Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG.

Wee See Boon stated that the payments to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG were in relation to services furnished to Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd from around 2014-03-01 to around 2016-09-01 concerning the vessel Blue Giant in the Gulf of Mexico.

Wee See Boon stated that he was informed by finance manager Ms Lim Woan Ching after the event that payments to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG had been made and that these payments were approved by the German finance department via an authorised digital token.

On 2020-09-11, Luther LLP sent a letter on behalf of the three former directors of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd, including Wee See Boon, explaining that service providers reserved the right to settle group-wide costs later and that the costs were settled in 2018 as part of a usual setup in the industry.
On 2021-06-15, I.N.C Law LLC sent a letter on behalf of Wee See Boon stating that it is not uncommon to distribute project-related costs among project sponsors at a later point in time.

On 2022-03-01, Wee See Boon, through his legal representatives, proposed that if restitution of interest was required, the applicable rate should be the then-prevailing fixed deposit interest rate of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd's bank of 0.8% per annum.

Wee See Boon stated that Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd was wound up due to business failure and that costs associated with the petitioning creditor and liquidator would have been incurred in any event.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on his role and knowledge as local resident director, Wee See Boon should bear personal responsibility for the December 2018 payments totaling US$1,472,500 made by Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG and for the claimed additional damages of interest and investigation-related costs.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors may be held personally responsible where their conduct in relation to company transactions and financial management does not meet the standards of directors' duties under company and insolvency law, including where they are involved in or fail to address transactions later set aside as preferential, but the assessment depends on their actual and expected involvement and knowledge in the circumstances.
Party_Details.Application: Wee See Boon was the local resident director and a bank signatory of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd and its immediate holding company, and he signed service agreements in October 2018 with HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG relating to earlier services for the vessel Blue Giant. The December 2018 payments to these entities were executed during a period when the company had ceased generating revenue and had significant liabilities, including a large debt to OIG Giant 1 Pte Ltd. According to his account, operational and financial decisions were made by the German directors, and he learned of the payments only after they were made, being told that they had been approved by the German finance department via an authorised token. He later participated in correspondence explaining the group practice of settling project-related and group-wide costs at a later stage and proposed a lower interest rate if restitution of interest were required. These circumstances frame the evaluation of whether his conduct, given his described role and knowledge, met the applicable standards for directors in relation to the payments and the claimed additional damages.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Wee See Boon, as a director, owed fiduciary duties and a duty of care, skill and diligence, but focused on whether his conduct in relation to the December 2018 payments and the claimed additional damages amounted to a breach of those duties. The judge noted that the plaintiff's case was built on the premise that the payments were unfair preferences and that this should translate into personal liability for the director, relying on authorities where directors who procured undue preferences were held to have breached fiduciary duties. The court distinguished those cases, particularly Living the Link, on the basis that in those situations the director had actively executed or procured the impugned transactions, whereas here the allegation against Wee See Boon was that he failed to prevent payments that he said he did not know about. The judge examined the evidence cited by the plaintiff, including the audited financial statements, the service agreements, and the 11 September 2020 letter, and concluded that while these documents showed that Wee See Boon knew of the existence of other creditors and of group arrangements for allocating costs, they did not demonstrate that he knew or should have known that the specific payments to HARPA Service & Support GmbH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG were made in December 2018. The court found that the plaintiff had not established actual knowledge of the payments and had not shown that the defendant was placed in a position where he ought reasonably to have discovered them. The judge accepted the explanation in the 11 September 2020 letter that the amounts due to the service providers were finalised and settled in 2018 as part of a group practice and rejected the suggestion that there was deliberate concealment of debts in earlier financial statements. The court held that even if the debts should have been recorded earlier, the plaintiff had not shown a causal link between any defect in the financial statements and the payments or the additional damages claimed. On the duty to act bona fide in the interests of the company and its creditors, the court held that the inference of breach from the existence of an unfair preference was not automatic and that, in the absence of evidence that Wee See Boon knew or should have known of the payments, his failure to intervene could not be characterised as a breach of that duty. On the duty of care, skill and diligence, the court considered the nature of his role as a local resident director with primarily administrative responsibilities and the fact that the German directors controlled operations and finances. Referring to case law recognising that the expected level of involvement of a local resident director may be more limited, the judge found that the plaintiff had not articulated what specific monitoring or oversight steps were reasonably required of Wee See Boon in the circumstances or how his conduct fell below the objective standard. The court also distinguished Ho Pak Kim, where the director was found to be actively involved and aware of the relevant transactions, and held that the reasoning there did not apply to a director whose knowledge of the impugned payments had not been established. The judge noted that the defendant had sought conversion of the originating summons to a writ to allow for a full trial with oral evidence, including evidence from the German directors, but that this was opposed by the plaintiff, and no cross-examination of the defendant was sought. On the available affidavit and documentary record, the court found that the plaintiff had not proved any breach of fiduciary or statutory duty by Wee See Boon and therefore rejected the claim for additional damages, including interest and investigation and petition costs, and dismissed the plaintiff's claim against him.
